blurb: Jewel-toned 'snapping' flower spikes kids love — and bumblebees pry open; the real garden snapdragon, not the noxious look-alike 'wild snapdragon' (yellow toadflax) that sneaks into seed mixes.
water: Moderate; ~1 in/week, water at the base
spread: Clumping; reseeds; cool-season (often overwinters in mild spots)
seasons: Spikes in nearly every color late spring–fall; best in cool weather
origin: Mediterranean — SW Europe (Spain) and NW Africa (Morocco)
habitat: Rocky cliffs, walls, and dry stony ground

care.hardiness: Tender perennial (USDA zones 7–10) grown as a cool-season annual on the Front Range (zones 5b–6a). Tolerates light frost, so plants can go in earlier than heat-lovers — transplant to the garden 2–3 weeks before the last average frost (late April in Boulder). They perform best in spring and early fall; summer heat causes them to pause or go dormant, and a hard freeze kills them.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade — 6+ hours for strongest flowering. In Boulder's intense high-altitude sun, afternoon shade helps snapdragons stay in bloom through the hottest weeks and prevents premature bolting.[1][2]
care.soil: Moist, organically rich, well-drained soil is ideal. Amend Colorado's alkaline clay-loam with compost at planting to improve both drainage and moisture retention. Slightly acidic to neutral pH (6.2–7.0). Avoid heavy clay that stays wet — root rots are the primary killer in waterlogged ground.[1]
care.water: Moderate and consistent — about 1 in per week. Water at the soil line to keep foliage dry; wet leaves promote the fungal diseases (rust, powdery mildew) that are snapdragons' main weakness. Drip irrigation or soaker hoses are ideal. In Colorado's low humidity and alkaline water, this is less of an issue than in humid climates, but good base hygiene still matters.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space transplants 6–12 in apart depending on cultivar size (compact dwarfs at 6 in, tall cut-flower types at 12 in). Good airflow between plants is important for disease prevention — do not crowd.[1][2]
care.sow: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost — around early-to-mid March for Boulder's mid-May frost date. Press seeds onto the surface of moist seed-starting mix and do not cover; snapdragons need light to germinate. Keep soil at 65–75°F; germination takes 7–14 days. Harden seedlings off over 7–10 days before transplanting.[1]
care.depth: Surface-sow — do not cover seeds. Just press firmly onto moistened seed-starting mix. Germination is best at 60–70°F with light exposure. Transplant into the garden at the same depth they were growing in the pot.[1]
care.bloom: Spring through early summer (May–July) on the Front Range, with a second flush in late summer through fall (September–October) once the heat breaks. Bloom stalls or stops in the hottest weeks of July–August. Long days of intense Colorado sun at altitude often produce vivid, well-saturated colors.[1][2]
care.feeding: Apply a balanced fertilizer every 2–4 weeks while plants are actively blooming. A moderate application at planting of compost or slow-release fertilizer is a good base. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which promotes leafy growth over flowering.[1]
care.maintenance: Pinch the growing tips of young transplants once or twice to encourage branching and a bushy, multi-stemmed plant. Deadhead spent spikes by snipping back to the nearest lateral bud to extend bloom. If plants go leggy or stop flowering in midsummer heat, cut back hard to 6 in — they will often resprout and rebloom vigorously in the cooler weeks of September. Tall cultivars may need light staking in wind. Remove and discard any plant that develops rust.[1][2]
care.selfsow: May self-sow in mild winters and lightly in sheltered spots; seedlings will not reliably match parent cultivar colors. Treat volunteers as a bonus rather than relying on them.[1]
care.troubles: Snapdragon rust is the most serious disease — yellow-orange pustules on leaf undersides. Choose rust-resistant cultivars when available. Remove and bag affected plants; do not compost. Rotate planting location the following year. Aphids and spider mites may appear in hot, dry spells; knock off with water or use insecticidal soap. Colorado's low humidity greatly reduces fungal disease pressure versus humid climates, but still water at the base.[1][2]
care.companions: Cool-season partners: pansies, violas, sweet alyssum, larkspur, and bachelor's button for spring. Transition to heat-tolerant neighbors (cosmos, zinnias, salvia) as summer sets in and snapdragons rest. Makes an excellent cut flower.[1]
care.planting: Snapdragons are cool-season plants: transplant hardened-off starts into the garden 2–3 weeks before the last average frost — late April in Boulder, when temperatures are still cool but hard freezes are unlikely. This cool-start advantage is one of their great strengths on the Front Range. If direct-sowing outdoors, sow in very early spring (late March–April) on the soil surface with no cover; germination is slow and unreliable in cold ground, so starting indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting (see 'sow') gives far better results. In containers, transplant at the same late-April window; pots dry out faster than in-ground beds, so check moisture daily in Boulder's wind and sun. Container snapdragons need no overwintering — they are killed by the first hard freeze in fall and the pot is cleared then.[1]
care.propagation: Seed is the standard method for home gardeners. Start indoors 8–10 weeks before the last frost (early-to-mid March for Boulder); surface-sow on moist seed-starting mix without covering, keep at 65–75°F, and expect germination in 7–14 days. Named cultivar colors will not come true from self-sown seed, so save seed only from open-pollinated varieties if color matters. Stem cuttings are possible for overwintering a particularly good plant: take 3–4 in tip cuttings in late summer, root in moist perlite or seed-starting mix under cover, and overwinter the rooted cuttings in a bright, frost-free spot (windowsill or cold frame) for replanting in spring — this is the easiest non-seed method to carry a favorite form over winter in Colorado's harsh climate.[1]

edible.summary: Snapdragon flowers are edible and non-toxic — but bitter, so they are used as a colorful garnish rather than eaten in quantity.[2][3]
edible.parts: Only the flowers are used. They are listed as an edible flower, employed to add color to salads, desserts, and plates as a garnish. The leaves, stems, and seed capsules are not eaten. There is no significant culinary tradition of eating snapdragon beyond the ornamental garnish use.[2]
edible.preparation: Use the petals fresh, raw, as a garnish — pick clean, unsprayed blooms and scatter them over a dish for color. Because the flavor is distinctly bitter, they are not eaten by the handful or cooked into food; a few flowers for decoration is the normal use.[2]
edible.caution: Snapdragon is non-toxic to people and, per the ASPCA, non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, so it poses no poisoning hazard. The only practical cautions are ordinary ones: the flowers are bitter, so don't expect them to taste good, and eating a large quantity of any plant can cause mild stomach upset — discourage children from grazing on them. Use only flowers you know are free of pesticides or floral preservatives.[2][3]
